Standardise CPR numbers

clean_cpr.Rd

Cleans a vector of Danish CPR numbers to the canonical 10-digit form: dashes and spaces are stripped, and nine-digit values consisting only of digits are zero-padded on the left. The padding is well-founded: birth days 01-09 mean that roughly a third of all CPR numbers legitimately start with a zero, which Excel silently drops from numeric cells.

Usage

clean_cpr(x)

Arguments

x

A character vector of CPR numbers. Numeric vectors are converted with as.character() first.

Value

A character vector of the same length.

Details

This is standardisation only – no validation. Values that cannot be brought to 10 digits are returned cleaned but otherwise untouched, so a malformed number stays visibly malformed for downstream checks. Use add_cpr_info() for validity, or pipe through a checkpoint (see vignette("validation")).

add_cpr_info() applies exactly this cleaning internally, so a column standardised with clean_cpr() round-trips unchanged.

See also

add_cpr_info(), clean_cvr()

Examples

clean_cpr(c("111111-1118", "1111111118", "101004007", NA))
#> [1] "1111111118" "1111111118" "0101004007" NA          

# Typical use: standardise the join key on both sides
# df  |> dplyr::mutate(pnr = clean_cpr(pnr))
